spec_match.rationale: Right column uses native Chrome 149 behaviour (real `text-overflow: ellipsis` inputs); left column simulates the pre-149 stickiness via JS that holds the ellipsis on focus. Three paired field types make the contrast legible.

interactive.rationale: Real focusable inputs, live focused/blurred state badges, three categories — visitor types and tabs through the comparison themselves.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Blurb promises side-by-side old vs new for URL, email, and search; that's exactly what the page renders.

edge_cases.rationale: Three latin-script field types but no RTL (Arabic, Hebrew) example — bidi behaviour around the focused-clip transition is one of the most interesting edge cases of the spec change. Also no password field (which has its own visual-clamp semantics).

design_system.rationale: Variables-only colours, but two unicode glyphs (`→` arrow markers) used in the comparison header; convention is inline SVG.

openQuestions.title: Add chromestatus reference link
openQuestions.detail: see-also lists no chromestatus.com/feature/5146265241387008 anchor — required by the self-heal invariant.

openQuestions.title: Add an RTL row
openQuestions.detail: Arabic and Hebrew with `text-overflow: ellipsis` clip from the start of the visual line, not the logical end. Add a paired Arabic field so the visitor can confirm the new behaviour respects bidi.

openQuestions.title: Show keyboard caret navigation explicitly
openQuestions.detail: Spec mentions caret navigation triggers the clip. Currently visitors trigger via click. Add a `press End / arrow keys to scroll` hint and a visible caret indicator.

openQuestions.title: Replace `→` arrows with inline SVG
openQuestions.detail: Two unicode arrows in the header; project rule is inline SVG.

summary: Strongest concept in the feature folder — real side-by-side, accurate simulation of legacy behaviour. Needs the chromestatus link and an RTL row to be the canonical reference.
